title: Blink an LED
intro: The classic first Arduino project: make an LED switch on and off once per second. It teaches you the upload cycle and basic digital output.
steps: Put the LED on the breadboard, with the two legs in different rows.
Connect a 220Ω resistor from the long leg's row to digital Pin 7.
Connect the short leg to a GND pin.
Upload the sketch. The LED should blink on and off every second.

title: Button-Controlled LED
intro: Read a push button and use it to control an LED. Uses the internal pull-up resistor so you don't need an extra one for the button.
steps: Place the button across the breadboard's centre gap.
Wire one side of the button to Pin 2, the other to GND.
Wire the LED: long leg through a 220Ω resistor to Pin 7, short leg to GND.
Upload the sketch. The LED lights only while the button is held.

title: Potentiometer LED Dimmer
intro: Turn a potentiometer to smoothly dim an LED. Introduces reading an analog input and writing a PWM output.
steps: Wire the potentiometer's outer pins to 5V and GND, and its middle pin to A0.
Wire the LED's long leg through a resistor to PWM Pin 9, short leg to GND.
Upload the sketch and turn the knob to change brightness.

title: Automatic Night Light
intro: An LED that turns on automatically when it gets dark, using a photoresistor in a voltage divider. Teaches the divider circuit and threshold logic.
wiring: 10kΩ resistor → from A0 junction to GND
steps: Build a voltage divider: photoresistor from 5V to A0, and a 10kΩ resistor from A0 to GND.
Wire the LED's long leg through a resistor to Pin 7, short leg to GND.
Upload the sketch and open the Serial Monitor (9600 baud) to see the light reading.
Cover the sensor: the LED should switch on. Tune the threshold value in code to match your room.

title: RGB Mood Lamp
intro: Cycle an RGB LED through colours by mixing red, green, and blue with PWM. Teaches how colour is made from three channels.
steps: Identify the common-cathode pin (usually the longest leg) and wire it to GND.
Wire each colour leg through its own 220Ω resistor to PWM Pins 9, 10, and 11.
Upload the sketch; the lamp cycles through colours. (If colours look inverted, you likely have a common-anode LED – wire the common leg to 5V instead.)

title: Piezo Melody Player
intro: Play a simple scale through a piezo buzzer using tone(). Teaches frequencies and looping over an array.
steps: Wire the buzzer's positive leg to Pin 8 and the other leg to GND.
Upload the sketch; you'll hear an eight-note scale repeat.
Try changing the frequency values to play your own tune.

title: Ultrasonic Parking Sensor
intro: A reverse-parking aid: an ultrasonic sensor measures the distance to the nearest object, and a buzzer beeps faster as you get closer. An LED gives a steady warning once you're too close.
steps: Place the HC-SR04, buzzer, and LED on the breadboard with room between them.
Wire the HC-SR04: VCC to 5V, GND to ground, Trig to Pin 9, Echo to Pin 10.
Connect the buzzer: positive leg to Pin 8, negative leg to ground.
Connect the LED: long leg through a 220Ω resistor to Pin 7, short leg to ground.
Upload the sketch.
Open the Serial Monitor at 9600 baud to watch the live distance, then move your hand toward the sensor to test the beep and LED.

title: Servo Sweep Control
intro: Sweep a servo motor back and forth between 0 and 180 degrees. Introduces the Servo library and controlling angle with a loop.
steps: Connect the servo's signal wire to Pin 9, power to 5V, and ground to GND.
Upload the sketch; the horn sweeps smoothly back and forth.
If the servo is weak or jittery, power it from a separate 5V supply sharing a common ground.

title: Temperature & Humidity Display
intro: Read temperature and humidity from a DHT11 and show them on a 16x2 I2C LCD. Combines a sensor library with I2C display output.
steps: Install the 'DHT sensor library' and 'LiquidCrystal I2C' libraries in the Arduino IDE.
Wire the DHT11 data pin to Pin 2; wire the LCD's SDA/SCL to your board's I2C pins.
Upload the sketch. Temperature and humidity appear on the LCD, updating every 2 seconds.
If the LCD stays blank, its I2C address may be 0x3F instead of 0x27 – change it in the code.

title: Motion-Activated Alarm
intro: A PIR motion sensor triggers a buzzer and LED when it detects movement. Teaches digital sensor input and simple state handling.
steps: Wire the PIR's OUT pin to Pin 2, with VCC to 5V and GND to ground.
Wire the buzzer to Pin 8 and the LED (through a resistor) to Pin 7.
Upload the sketch and open the Serial Monitor (9600 baud).
Wave your hand: the buzzer and LED activate. Use the PIR's trim pots to adjust sensitivity and delay.

title: Reaction Time Game
intro: Test your reflexes: after a random delay the LED lights, and the game measures how fast you press the button. Teaches millis() timing and randomness.
steps: Wire the button to Pin 2/GND, the LED (via resistor) to Pin 7, and the buzzer to Pin 8.
Upload the sketch and open the Serial Monitor (9600 baud).
Wait for the LED to light, then press the button as fast as you can.
Your reaction time in milliseconds prints to the monitor after each round.

title: Ultrasonic Distance Display
intro: Show live distance from an ultrasonic sensor on an I2C LCD. Combines pulse-timing measurement with formatted display output.
steps: Install the 'LiquidCrystal I2C' library.
Wire the HC-SR04 (Trig to Pin 9, Echo to Pin 10) and the LCD to the I2C pins.
Upload the sketch; the measured distance updates on the LCD a few times per second.
If the LCD is blank, change the I2C address from 0x27 to 0x3F.

title: Motion-Triggered Servo Door
intro: A PIR sensor opens a servo-driven 'door' when it detects motion, then closes it after a few seconds. Teaches chaining a sensor input to an actuator output.
steps: Wire the PIR's OUT pin to Pin 2; wire the servo's signal to Pin 9 with power and ground.
Upload the sketch. The servo starts at the closed position.
Trigger the PIR: the servo swings to open, holds for 3 seconds, then closes.
If the servo causes resets, power it from a separate 5V supply with a shared ground.

title: WiFi Weather Logger
intro: Read temperature and humidity on an ESP32 and send each reading to a web endpoint over WiFi. Teaches connecting to WiFi and making HTTP requests.
steps: Install the 'DHT sensor library' and select your ESP32 board in the Arduino IDE.
Fill in your WiFi name and password, and replace the endpoint URL with your own logging service (e.g. a Google Sheets webhook or your API).
Wire the DHT11 data pin to GPIO4.
Upload and open the Serial Monitor (115200 baud) to confirm it connects and posts a reading each minute.

title: Web-Controlled LED
intro: Host a tiny web page on the ESP32 to switch an LED on and off from your phone or laptop. Teaches running a web server and routing HTTP requests to GPIO.
steps: Select your ESP32 board in the Arduino IDE and fill in your WiFi name and password.
Wire the LED's long leg through a resistor to GPIO2, short leg to GND.
Upload and open the Serial Monitor (115200 baud) to read the ESP32's IP address.
Open that IP in a browser on the same network and use the ON/OFF links to control the LED.
